so the reason I thought it would be nice to call the failure callback is because of our workflow, I'll describe it here but I'm pretty sure it's generic:
- we want to do modifications by batch on 20 objects
- we have a failure callback that does something on the objects (in detail, it sets an error on the object for error handling later)
- we want to implement a retry mechanism on transient issue, BUT we still want to mark our object as errored, in order to signal it to the user
- later an async task will retry the transient failures. At the time of the retry (other thread) there is no trace of the previous objects.

Does that make sense? In that case, could we call the failure callbacks with for empty responses?

Hi,

That's fine by me. My reasoning for using a list of tuples was in case the
sequencing of the headers was important to a user for some reason, but I
agree a dictionary is a simpler approach. Could we use an ordered
dictionary maybe?

I just had a quick look at the HTTP spec and it seems the ordering of
headers is not significant, except in the slightly more edge case of where
multiple headers with the same name are present:

"The order in which header fields with differing field names are received
is not significant. However, it is "good practice" to send general-header
fields first, followed by request-header or response- header fields, and
ending with the entity-header fields.

Multiple message-header fields with the same field-name MAY be present in a
message if and only if the entire field-value for that header field is
defined as a comma-separated list [i.e., #(values)]. It MUST be possible to
combine the multiple header fields into one "field-name: field-value" pair,
without changing the semantics of the message, by appending each subsequent
field-value to the first, each separated by a comma. The order in which
header fields with the same field-name are received is therefore
significant to the interpretation of the combined field value, and thus a
proxy MUST NOT change the order of these field values when a message is
forwarded."

( http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#page-31 )

It's your call, what shall we go with, list of tuples, ordered dictionary
or plain dictionary?

Dan

That's right, and I didn't mean to imply comments aren't legal (on their own line) or are an awful practice in `requirements.txt`. In the vein of "making it harder to break documentation by accident", using `lines` seems more robust and understandable than `start`/`end` for `requirements.txt`, because:

1. using `start`/`end` to include `requirements.txt` relies on the file using a package with a specific name, unless you use comments to mark the section to include.  But if `requirements.txt` is generated, e.g. with `pip freeze`, you have to remember to add the comments back when using `start`/`end`.
2.  *usually* there's no specific section of the file we'd want to draw attention to -- e.g. when we `!inc` this repo's `requirements.txt`, we only care that it shows some section of the file and that it's not too long. If we used comment markers and `start`/`end`, the amount of the file included in the docs might silently become much longer just by adding packages. This isn't "breaking the docs", but it's probably not desired.

One situation where `lines` should *not* be used is if we *wanted* to include a specific section, for example if we knew that a specific update of some package we use has a bug. In that case, we could add a comment above the line of `requirements.txt` which pins that package so that nobody bumps the version. If we wanted to include that specific part of `requirements.txt` in documentation, we could and definitely should use `start`/`end` so that we could make other changes to the file without breaking the docs.

I'll describe when `lines` vs `start`/`end` should be used and why in more depth here.

Can you add a bit more information about how this would be used? We would generally prefer that people explicitly declare the dependencies of a target, which provides enough information to infer the include paths automatically.

So on the surface, I don't think this option makes sense: it would bypass change detection and allow a target to depend on an entire other subdirectory without declaring it as a dependency. In essence, it would exacerbate the internal ticket we have about this: `DPB-5639`, which states:
```
Currently, scrooge gen adds the source root of targets to the import paths of the global scrooge gen. This means that a target that depends on a thrift file [under a particular source root] can include any thrift [under that source root].

When there is an include in a thrift file whose target doesn't have matching dependency listings, incompatible changes to the included file can pass SQ, but will cause subsequent builds to fail.
```

And in general, every time you add a class to the codebase, you add complexity - docs that need to be maintained, more things to know about when writing code, and more things to modify if we want to refactor the code later.  Sometimes that complexity is definitely worth it.  But for really simple things, like in the two abstractions you've added here, the cost in additional complexity isn't worth the benefit that you get from using the abstraction.  We need to be really careful to not just add abstractions for abstraction's sake.

My general rule of thumb is the following loose collection of axioms and guidelines.

- classes are typically stateful and statefullness is hard to manage.
- functions (especially pure ones) are easier to reason about than classes.
- a class with only one function is just a function disguised as a class.  This also regularly applies to classes with only 2-3 functions.
- a method that doesn't use `self` should almost always be a function.
- it is *ok* to include convenience functions using `@staticmethod`, but normally should only be done if the convenience is *external*, meaning that outside actors who use this class almost always need the convenience function.  If it's only needed internally, it should just be used as a stand-alone function.

@martindurant I think we are missing the purpose of including the offset parameter in the first place. It is strictly forbidden to append at any offset other than the very end of the file. You will get an exception if you try to append:
a) an index where there is already data
b) at an index past the end of the file.

The purpose of utilizing the offset parameter is to protect the multi-threaded upload portion from inadvertently uploading too much data without just failing out. Here is the specific scenario we are trying to protect against:
1. multi-part uploader begins upload 1GB file
2. while appending a 4mb block the front end doesn't respond back, so we cancel the connection and start the retry logic
3. What we don't know is that the append succeeded in the back end. The data is there and in the file.
4. If we append now (as a retry) without including the offset we expect the file to be at, then the append will succeed (again) and we will get an extra 4mb of data in the file.

The solution for this particular problem, is that applications that use the client can protect themselves from this scenario by explicitly specifying the offset they want, then making a decision about what to do if they get a badoffset exception.

The client itself should only give the caller options, it should not make any decisions for the caller directly.

```python
with self.assertRaises(errors.Error):
```

or

```
self.assertRaises(errors.Error, self.client.obtain_certificate, domains)
```

Yeah, the time zone stuff needs a pretty serious overhaul. The reason I haven't really started into it totally yet is that I think it involves some opportunistic loading of time zone data from various sources, and I'm not sure how heavy a burden that will be in resource-constrained environments, so I've been trying to think through the right way to do it.

I'm not sure why the between-parens tzname criteria is different, but one thing I've learned is that a lot of the stuff that just seems crazy in the parser is there because there's some spec or some subset of people generating date strings in a very specific way (like the fact that `12:00:00+0300` and '12:00:00 UTC+0300` parse to UTC + 3 and UTC **-3**, respectively), so it's probably worth trying to dig up why they were separate in the first place before unifying them.

That said, we should definitely maximize code-reuse if only so that downstream users only have to patch in a single place if they want a different behavior, so if there's some reason they are different, it's worth factoring out everything except that difference.

With `@property` you create a new property replacing the inherited one, with what I used here you overwrite the getter of the inherited property. Both works in this case: as the base class only defines a getter *for now*, we could also just redefine the property as a whole. I guess the question generally boils down to what you want to happen in the subclasses if the base class ever introduces a setter (or deleter): Should they inherit it or not? We'll arguably never introduce a setter for the output shape, so it still doesn't matter which way we choose. I chose this one because it explicitly tells that we're overriding a property that's also present in the base class.

Perhaps, but for the call to `configure()`, I thought it best to use the explicit `Ganga.Utility.Config.configure()` rather than just `configure()` to make it clear *what* is being configured.

It becomes the difference between:
```python
from Ganga.Utility.Config import getConfig, configure

system_vars = {}
for opt in getConfig('System'):
    system_vars[opt] = getConfig('System')[opt]
configure([os.path.expanduser('~/.gangarc')], system_vars)  # This is ambiguous in my view
```
and
```python
import Ganga.Utility.Config

system_vars = {}
for opt in Ganga.Utility.Config.getConfig('System'):
    system_vars[opt] = Ganga.Utility.Config.getConfig('System')[opt]
Ganga.Utility.Config.configure([os.path.expanduser('~/.gangarc')], system_vars)
```

Perhaps a compromise would be:
```python
from Ganga.Utility import Config

system_vars = {}
for opt in Config.getConfig('System'):
    system_vars[opt] = Config.getConfig('System')[opt]
Config.configure([os.path.expanduser('~/.gangarc')], system_vars)
```
or
```python
from Ganga.Utility.Config import getConfig
import Ganga.Utility.Config

system_vars = {}
for opt in getConfig('System'):
    system_vars[opt] = getConfig('System')[opt]
Ganga.Utility.Config.configure([os.path.expanduser('~/.gangarc')], system_vars)
```

Instead of adding a new task, we could add the ping logic in the `_send_queued_message`:

- Send ping to check if partner is online
    - if ping unconfirmed -> node is unavailable
        - wait for partner to come online (if any message is received from partner)
- loop:
  - if there is a message in the queue
    - until message confirmed, have retries, or partner is back online
       - send message
       - wait for confirmation or timeout
       - if max_retries == 0 node is unavailable (the queue message worked and the Ping)
          - wait for partner to come back online (available if any message is received from partner)
          - restart max_retries counter
        - elseif unconfirmed, decrement max_retries
  - if no message in the queue and `send-ping-time` passed
    - send Ping and wait for confirmation
    - if ping unconfirmed -> node is unavailable
        - wait for partner to come back online (available if any message is received from partner)

This would make one task per channel, we eliminate synchronizations issues for messages that have `max_retries`, and we can start these queues in a randomization loop as suggested by @konradkonrad
